Elihu has been speaking throughout these chapters, and now he reaches his crescendo—not with another round of accusations against Job, but with something far more arresting. He pivots from debate to witness. His heart pounds. His breath catches. He has glimpsed something true about God that words can barely contain, and he demands that Job see it too. This is not the voice of a theologian composing careful arguments. This is the voice of someone genuinely undone by the majesty of God's power. Thunder rolls across the sky, lightning splits the heavens, snow falls at God's command, ice forms by His breath—and in these ordinary displays of weather, Elihu sees the signature of a power so vast that it makes human categories of justice and explanation seem almost quaint. God does not explain Himself to the storm. He simply speaks, and it obeys.

What makes this passage cut so deep is that Elihu is not using nature as a poetic metaphor for God's goodness. He is doing something more unsettling: he is showing Job that God's power operates entirely independent of whether Job understands it or approves of it. The clouds swirl at God's direction. The lightning accomplishes what He commands. Whether it comes for punishment or for blessing, it all moves according to His will and His loving devotion—a phrase that should stop us cold. Elihu is saying that God's control over creation is absolute, and this same God, who needs no permission to thunder, certainly needs no permission to allow Job to suffer. The implicit challenge is devastating: if you cannot comprehend how God makes snow fall or ice form, how can you possibly comprehend the justice of your own affliction?

Yet notice what Elihu does not say. He does not say God is cruel or indifferent. He says explicitly that "in His justice and great righteousness He does not oppress." This is the hardest truth for Job to accept—that God's power and His justice are not in conflict, that the very God who commands the lightning is also the God who does not abuse His power. Elihu is calling Job to a posture of trust not because everything makes sense, but because God's character is trustworthy precisely when nothing makes sense. To fear God, to stand in awe before Him, is not a retreat into ignorance; it is the only rational response to a Being whose knowledge is perfect and whose power cannot be questioned.

The reflection leaves us where Elihu intends: silenced. Job wanted an explanation. Instead, he is given a vision of the One to whom explanations are not owed. When we come to God with our suffering and our questions, demanding that He justify Himself to our understanding, we are asking Him to shrink Himself to human proportions. What Elihu offers—what God is preparing to offer in the chapters ahead—is something far stranger and more terrible and more wonderful: an encounter with God's actual bigness. Not answers that fit our boxes, but a presence so overwhelming that we stop asking whether our boxes were ever the right size.
